a: A custom exception is created by extending Exception or RuntimeException. Helps define application-specific error scenarios clearly. Can include custom constructors to pass messages or other details. Promotes code reuse and better exception handling practices. Can be used in both checked and unchecked exception scenarios.

Feature | Shallow Copy | Deep Copy
Object Reference | Copies reference of objects | Creates new instances for all fields
Memory Usage | Less memory used | More memory as new objects are created
Mutability | Changes reflect in all copies | Changes are isolated
Performance | Faster | Slower

String is an immutable sequence of characters. Stored in the String Constant Pool for memory efficiency. Once created, its value cannot be changed. Any change creates a new string object. It is thread-safe due to immutability. Useful for keys and constants where consistent value is needed.

Feature | String | StringBuffer
Mutability | Immutable | Mutable
Thread Safety | Thread-safe (by nature) | Thread-safe (synchronized methods)
Performance | Slower for concatenation | Faster for frequent changes
Use Case | Fixed or rarely changed strings | When string modification is frequent

Feature | Thread-safe | Non-thread-safe
Concurrency | Safe to use across multiple threads | Not safe without external synchronization
Example | Vector, Hashtable | ArrayList, HashMap
Performance | Slower due to synchronization | Faster in single-threaded environments
Usage | Multithreading scenarios | Simple, non-concurrent tasks

Automatically deallocates memory by destroying unused objects. Helps prevent memory leaks and out-of-memory errors. Runs as a background thread in the JVM. Uses algorithms like mark-and-sweep, generational GC. Improves overall memory efficiency in Java apps. Developers can suggest it using System.gc().

Feature | ArrayList | LinkedList
Data Structure | Dynamic array | Doubly linked list
Access Time | Fast (index-based) | Slow (traversal needed)
Insertion/Deletion | Slow (shifting needed) | Fast (pointer adjustment)
Memory Usage | Less memory | More memory (pointers)

Feature | Set | Map
Structure | Collection of unique elements | Key-value pair structure
Duplicates | No duplicate elements | Keys are unique, values can duplicate
Access | Only elements | Access by keys
Common Implementations | HashSet, LinkedHashSet | HashMap, TreeMap

Lambda Expressions: Enables functional programming by passing behavior as data. Stream API: Supports bulk operations on collections using a pipeline approach. Functional Interfaces: Interfaces with a single abstract method (e.g., Runnable, Comparator). Optional: A container object to avoid null references and NullPointerException. Default Methods: Allows interface methods with default implementation. Date & Time API: Modern replacement for java.util.Date with immutability and better handling.

Introduced in Java 8 to avoid null checks and NullPointerException. Represents a value that may or may not be present. Provides methods like isPresent(), orElse(), and ifPresent(). Encourages functional-style programming using map() and filter(). Promotes cleaner and safer code with null handling.

Encapsulation: Wrapping of data and methods into a single unit (class). Abstraction: Hiding internal implementation details and exposing only functionality. Inheritance: Enables code reuse by inheriting properties from a base class. Polymorphism: Allows multiple forms via method overloading/overriding. These principles are the foundation of object-oriented programming in Java.

A thread is the smallest unit of execution in a process. Java supports multithreading to run tasks concurrently. Reduces CPU idle time and improves application responsiveness. Threads can be created by extending Thread or implementing Runnable. Managed by JVM thread scheduler.

Feature | Aggregation | Composition
Relationship | Has-a | Part-of
Dependency | Weak association | Strong association
Lifecycle | Child can exist independently | Child's lifecycle depends on parent
Example | Department has students | House has rooms

q: What is Embedded DB and Embedded Server?
a: Embedded DB: A lightweight database (like H2) that runs inside the app process. Embedded Server: A server (e.g., Tomcat) bundled with the app; no need to deploy externally. Common in Spring Boot applications. Great for testing or small-scale deployments. No external configuration required to start the app.

Feature | Hashtable | HashMap
Thread Safety | Thread-safe (synchronized) | Not thread-safe
Null Keys/Values | No null keys or values allowed | One null key, multiple null values allowed
Performance | Slower due to locking | Faster in single-threaded contexts
Usage | Legacy systems | Modern applications

q: What is ConcurrentHashMap? Why use it?
a: A thread-safe alternative to HashMap introduced in Java 5. Uses segment-based locking to reduce contention. Allows concurrent read and write operations. Prevents ConcurrentModificationException. Ideal for high-concurrency multi-threaded environments.

q: Intermediate vs Terminal Operations in Stream
a:
Aspect | Intermediate | Terminal
Return Type | Returns a Stream | Returns a non-Stream result
Chaining | Supports method chaining | Ends the stream pipeline
Examples | map(), filter() | collect(), count()
Execution | Lazy | Eager

Feature | HashMap | LinkedHashMap
Ordering | No order maintained | Maintains insertion order
Performance | Faster (no order overhead) | Slightly slower
Use Case | Unordered fast access | Ordered iteration needed
Underlying Structure | Hash buckets | Hash + doubly-linked list

Feature | HashMap | ConcurrentHashMap
Thread Safety | Not thread-safe | Thread-safe
Concurrency Mechanism | No locking | Segment-level locking
Performance | Faster in single-threaded | Better for multi-threaded
Usage | General purpose | High-concurrency applications

q: How to store multiple types in ArrayList?
a: Use raw type or declare List<Object> to store different types. Java supports storing heterogeneous data via Object references. You lose type safety and need to cast when retrieving elements. Better alternatives include using wrapper classes or generics.

You can declare an exception using throws in the method signature. Used when the calling method should handle the exception. Common in IO operations and checked exceptions. Syntax: public void method() throws IOException. Helps propagate the exception to higher layers.

Extend Thread and override run() method. Or implement Runnable and pass to a Thread instance. Start the thread using start() method. Example: Thread t = new Thread(new MyRunnable()); t.start(); Prefer Runnable for better separation of logic and thread behavior.

A synchronized block ensures only one thread accesses the block at a time. Prevents race conditions and data inconsistency. Syntax: synchronized (object) { // code block }. Can synchronize on this or a specific object. Offers better control than synchronizing entire methods.

Extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked). Define constructors to pass messages or causes. Helps represent domain-specific issues clearly. Example: throw new CustomException("Invalid input");

Aspect | throw | throws
Usage | Used to actually throw an exception | Used to declare possible exceptions
Location | Inside method body | Method signature
Followed By | Instance of Exception | Exception class name(s)
Example | throw new IOException() | public void read() throws IOException

q: Which Set collection stores insertion order?
a: LinkedHashSet maintains the order of elements as inserted. Based on HashTable + linked list implementation. Useful when both uniqueness and order are important. Slower than HashSet but predictable iteration.

List types like ArrayList and LinkedList allow duplicates. In contrast, Set types do not allow duplicates. Useful when maintaining all values including repeats is important. Order can also be preserved depending on the List implementation.

Aspect | == | .equals()
Type | Operator | Method
Comparison | Memory reference | Content/Value
Overridable | No | Yes
Usage | Check identity | Check logical equality
None. All Map implementations in Java disallow duplicate keys. If a key is re-inserted, the value is replaced. Duplicate values are allowed but keys must be unique.

An interface for managing thread pools in Java. Provides methods like submit(), invokeAll() for task execution. Helps manage concurrency in a more scalable way than creating threads manually. Supports graceful shutdown and future-based async results.

A special memory region in Java heap storing interned string literals. Helps reduce memory usage and improves performance. Strings with the same content point to the same memory address. Created automatically by JVM when using "text" or intern().

Function | map() | flatMap()
Behavior | Applies function and returns a Stream of values | Flattens nested Streams into a single one
Use Case | One-to-one mapping | One-to-many mapping
Return Type | Stream<Stream<T>> | Stream<T>
Example | list.stream().map() | list.stream().flatMap()

Keyword | volatile | transient
Purpose | Ensures visibility across threads | Prevents serialization of a field
Usage | Multithreading | Serialization
Persistence | Part of object state | Skipped during serialization

A marker interface to support object cloning using clone(). Must override clone() from Object class. Enables shallow copy by default. Deep copy needs manual implementation. Throws CloneNotSupportedException if not implemented.

An interface with no methods or fields. Used to signal or tag a class for special treatment. Examples include Serializable, Clonable, Remote. JVM or frameworks detect marker interfaces using reflection.

Aspect | Runnable | Clonable
Purpose | Enable multithreading | Enable object cloning
Methods | run() | clone() (from Object)
Marker Interface | No | Yes

Feature | collect(Collectors.toList()) | toList()
Java Version | Java 8+ | Java 16+
Mutability | Mutable | Immutable
Type | ArrayList | Immutable List
Modifiability | Can modify | Cannot modify

q: Explain the OOPS main features such as encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism
a:
Encapsulation: Combines data and methods in a single unit (class), hiding internal implementation using access modifiers like private/protected.
Inheritance: Enables child classes to inherit fields and methods from parent classes, promoting code reuse and hierarchical relationships.
Polymorphism: Allows the same method to behave differently based on object type. Supports method overloading and overriding.
Abstraction: Hides internal implementation and shows only functionality through interfaces or abstract classes.

Feature | Collection | Stream
Storage | Stores elements in memory | Does not store data, only processes data
Processing | External iteration (using loops) | Internal iteration (pipeline)
Consumption | Reusable | One-time use
Parallelism | Manual | Built-in via parallel streams

q: Why we need to use stream instead of collection?
a: Stream API enables functional-style operations for clean and declarative code. Supports lazy evaluation and chaining with intermediate operations. Can be parallelized to improve performance on large datasets. Does not modify the original collection, ensuring data immutability.

Streams are created using Collection.stream() or Stream.of(). Under the hood, Spliterator is used for iteration support. Intermediate operations build a pipeline of lazy-evaluated steps. Pipeline is executed only when a terminal operation (e.g., collect()) is called.

Lambda Expressions for writing concise function implementations. Stream API for functional operations on collections. Functional Interfaces with @FunctionalInterface annotation. Optional class for avoiding null pointer exceptions. Default & Static methods in interfaces for backward compatibility. New Date and Time API under java.time package (e.g., LocalDate).

Optional is a container object used to contain not-null objects. Avoids NullPointerException by safely representing null values. Provides methods like isPresent(), orElse(), ifPresent(). Encourages better null checks and functional-style programming.

q: Is datetime class mutable in java8?
a: No, DateTime classes like LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime are immutable. They return new objects on operations like plusDays(), ensuring thread safety. Prevents unintentional modifications and bugs in multithreaded environments. Immutable design improves predictability and functional programming compatibility.

Feature | Stream | Parallel Stream
Execution | Sequential (single thread) | Concurrent (multiple threads)
Performance | Better for small tasks | Better for large data sets
Thread safety | Not a concern | Requires caution with shared state
Control | More predictable | Less predictable order

Uses the ForkJoinPool under the hood (default common pool). Splits data into smaller parts using Spliterator. Processes each subtask in a separate thread concurrently. Combines partial results into the final result after execution.

Use when working with large datasets that are CPU-intensive. Use only for stateless, independent operations. Avoid if task is IO-bound or involves shared mutable state. Helps utilize multi-core CPU for better performance.

Terminal operation triggers execution of the stream pipeline. Examples: forEach(), collect(), reduce(). Produces a non-stream result (value, collection, side effect). After terminal operation, the stream is considered consumed.

q: Can we use method reference for instance methods?
a: Yes, instance methods can be referenced using object::methodName. Must be compatible with the target functional interface method signature. Improves code readability and reusability. Often used with stream operations and lambda expressions.

An interface with exactly one abstract method. Supports lambda expressions and method references. May contain default and static methods. Annotated with @FunctionalInterface (optional but recommended). Examples: Runnable, Callable, Function.

q: Is functional interface exist before java 8?
a: Yes, interfaces like Runnable and Comparator existed before Java 8. They had one abstract method but weren't formally called functional interfaces. Java 8 introduced the @FunctionalInterface annotation for better clarity.

q: Create a functional interface and add implementation?
a: @FunctionalInterface interface MyFunctionalInterface { void execute(); } public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { MyFunctionalInterface impl = () -> System.out.println("Implemented!"); impl.execute(); } }

q: Why functional interface because previously anonymous class do the same kind of functionality?
a: Functional interfaces simplify code by enabling lambda expressions, reducing verbosity compared to anonymous classes.
Example: Collectors.groupingBy(Person::getAge) groups a list of people by their age.

q: Count each letter count with java 8?
a: String input = "hello"; Map<Character, Long> result = input.chars().mapToObj(c -> (char)c).collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Function.identity(), Collectors.counting()));

q: Collect the duplicate elements in the list with java 8?
a: List<Integer> list = Arrays.asList(1, 2, 2, 3, 3); Set<Integer> duplicates = list.stream().filter(i -> Collections.frequency(list, i) > 1).collect(Collectors.toSet());
List<String> list = Arrays.asList("b", "a", "c"); list.sort(Comparator.naturalOrder());

q: Get the employee name list from the list of employees?
a: List<String> names = employees.stream().map(Employee::getName).collect(Collectors.toList());
List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3, 4, 5); numbers.parallelStream().forEach(System.out::println);
Static initializers are used to initialize static variables or perform setup tasks when the class is loaded.

q: How to create custom exception class?
a: class CustomException extends Exception { public CustomException(String message) { super(message); } }

q: Exception classes A and B extends A, can we throw checked exception from B and unchecked exception from A (vice versa)?
a: Yes. Exception type doesn't depend on class inheritance. Checked and unchecked exceptions behave independently. Any subclass can throw its own exception type. Checked exceptions must be declared or handled, regardless of hierarchy. Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) can be thrown freely without declaration. Hierarchy only affects catch blocks.

q: When do we need to invoke finalize method?
a: finalize() is called by GC before reclaiming memory of unreachable objects. Its use is discouraged due to unpredictability and poor performance. Instead, use try-with-resources or explicit cleanup methods. Finalizers may cause memory retention and unpredictability in modern JVMs. Deprecated in recent Java versions (after Java 8).

q: When do we use finally block?
a: To run cleanup code regardless of exceptions. Executes after try or catch blocks. Useful for releasing non-AutoCloseable resources (e.g., streams). Ensures consistency even if return or exception occurs. Can be omitted with try-with-resources for AutoCloseable cases.

Java construct that auto-closes resources at block end. Resources must implement AutoCloseable or Closeable. Auto-generates finally block to call close(). Reduces boilerplate and potential resource leaks. Supports multiple resources in one try with comma-separated list.

q: How to make a custom class eligible as a resource to use in try with resources?
a: Implement AutoCloseable or Closeable. Override the close() method for cleanup logic. Ensure no exceptions escape close or declare throws. Use it in try declaration: try (MyRes r = new MyRes()) {...}. The close() will be invoked automatically after try block.

== compares object references (memory addresses). equals() compares logical equality (content), if overridden. Default equals() in Object is same as ==. Override equals (and hashCode) in value-based classes (String, custom types). Use == for primitives; use equals for object values.

Wrap primitive types as objects (e.g., int -> Integer). Needed for use in collections (generics require objects). Provide utility methods: parsing, converting, and comparison. Support autoboxing/unboxing since Java 5. Allow null representation and sophistication beyond primitives.

volatile ensures that changes to a variable are visible across threads. Reads and writes bypass CPU caches and use main memory. Suitable for flags or simple state markers. Does not guarantee atomicity for compound actions. Use synchronized or Atomic types for complex operations.

q: What is the algorithm used inside Arrays.sort?
a: For primitive arrays: Dual-Pivot QuickSort (fast average performance). For object arrays: TimSort (combines merge + insertion sort). Optimized for partially ordered data (natural runs detection). Ensures stability for object sorting (equal elements retain order). Implemented in java.util.Arrays since Java 7+.

List: Ordered collection, allows duplicates, index-based access. Set: Unordered, unique elements, no duplicates. Map: Stores key-value pairs, keys unique but values can repeat. Choose List for sequence, Set for uniqueness, Map for key-based lookup. Implementations: ArrayList, HashSet, HashMap, etc.

q: Concurrent hashmap vs hash map difference?
a: HashMap: Not thread-safe, risk of corruption in concurrent use. ConcurrentHashMap: Thread-safe via finer-grained locking. Uses lock striping or segment-level locking per bucket. Allows concurrent reads and writes with high throughput. Null keys/values not allowed in ConcurrentHashMap (allowed in HashMap).

Uses an array of buckets; each bucket is a linked list or tree (for collisions). Computes index via hashCode(key) & (n-1). On collision, chains entries or converts to a balanced tree (if threshold exceeded). Resizes when load factor threshold (default 0.75) is exceeded. Provides O(1) average-case insert, lookup, and delete.

q: How index is calculated in hashmap?
a: Compute hash = key.hashCode(), then apply bit-mixing. Index = hash & (capacity - 1) (when capacity is power of two). This ensures uniform distribution across buckets. Reduces collisions and improves performance. Critical for internal optimization and scalability.

q: How concurrent hashmap works for multithread?
a: Partitions data into segments or buckets with separate locks. Readers access data without locking; writers lock only segment. Reduces contention and improves parallel throughput. Uses lock striping or synchronized bins depending on Java version. Avoids whole-map locking as seen in Hashtable.

q: Which operation is locked in concurrent hashmap?
a: Write operations are locked at the segment level.

q: What is the mechanism of locking happens in concurrent hashmap?
a: Uses fine-grained locking (lock striping) to minimize contention.

q: What happens if more than one thread try to insert into hashmap?
a: Without synchronization, data corruption or infinite loops can occur. Buckets or chains may break leading to lost entries. May trigger race conditions during table resize. Use ConcurrentHashMap or synchronized wrapper to avoid issues. HashMap alone is unsafe in multithreaded environments.

HashSet: backed by a HashMap internally; uses hash buckets. LinkedHashSet: maintains insertion order via linked list in each bucket. TreeSet: backed by TreeMap, stores elements in sorted order. Provides O(1) average performance for add, remove, contains (HashSet). Different implementations suit different use-cases.

Insert, delete, lookup: O(1) on average. Worst-case (many collisions): O(n), but rare with good hashing. Resizing takes O(n), amortized cost remains O(1). Using balanced tree (in Java 8+) avoids worst-case performance.

q: What is the data structure used for bucket in the hashmap?
a: Buckets are initially linked lists. After threshold, converted to balanced trees (TreeNode) for performance. Keeps insertion, lookup efficient under high load. Combination ensures O(1) average performance and O(log n) worst-case.

Aggregation: Weak has-a relationship. Child can live independently (e.g., Department & Student). Composition: Strong has-a relationship. Child's lifecycle bound to parent (e.g., House & Room). Composition implies ownership; aggregation implies association. Composition uses containment; aggregation uses references. Aggregation allows reuse; composition creates tight coupling.

q: What is List, Set, and Map?
a: List: Ordered, allows duplicates, access by index. Set: Unordered or ordered depending on implementation, no duplicates. Map: Key-value pairs, keys unique, values can duplicate. Choose List for sequences, Set for uniqueness, Map for key-based lookup. Implementations include ArrayList, HashSet, LinkedHashMap, TreeMap, etc.

q: What are the differences between List and Set?
a: List: Allows duplicates, maintains insertion order, index access. Set: No duplicates, no guaranteed order (unless LinkedHashSet/TreeSet). List is good for ordered data with repetition; Set for unique collections. Set implementations have faster membership tests by hash or tree.

q: What are the differences between Hashtable, HashMap, and TreeMap?
a: Hashtable: Thread-safe (synchronized), no null keys/values, legacy class. HashMap: Not thread-safe, allows one null key and null values. TreeMap: Sorted by key, implements NavigableMap, slower due to tree structure. Choose Hashtable for thread safety, HashMap for general use, TreeMap for sorted keys.

q: What is serialization and when it will be used?
a: Process of converting an object into byte stream for storage or transmission. Used in RMI, web sessions, caching, and file/persistence operations. Classes must implement Serializable marker interface. Use transient keyword to skip non-serializable fields. Deserialization recreates the object graph from the byte stream.

q: What are the differences between an interface and an abstract class?
a: Interface: Defines behavior but no implementation (until Java 7). Supports multiple inheritance. Cannot have constructors. All methods are abstract by default (Java 7 and below). Fields are public, static, and final by default. Abstract Class: Can define both behavior and implementation. Supports only single inheritance. Can have constructors. Can have abstract and non-abstract methods. Fields can be any access modifier and not necessarily final.

q: What is mutable and immutable? Give some examples
a: Mutable: Objects whose state can be changed after creation (e.g., StringBuilder, ArrayList). Immutable: Objects whose state cannot be changed once created (e.g., String, Integer). Immutability ensures thread-safety and predictability. Mutables are preferred when frequent modifications are needed.

q: What are the differences between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
a: String: Immutable and thread-safe. Any operation creates a new object. StringBuilder: Mutable and not thread-safe. Faster for single-threaded tasks. StringBuffer: Mutable and thread-safe via synchronized methods.

q: What are the differences between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?
a: HashMap: Not thread-safe. Concurrent modifications may cause data corruption. ConcurrentHashMap: Thread-safe. Uses lock striping or bucket-level locking.

q: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
a: Fail-Fast: Throws ConcurrentModificationException if modified during iteration (e.g., ArrayList, HashMap). Fail-Safe: Works on a cloned copy and avoids exceptions (e.g., ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList).

q: What are the features introduced in Java 8?
a: Lambda Expressions: Enables functional-style code by passing behavior as parameters. Stream API: Facilitates functional-style operations on collections. Functional Interfaces: Interfaces with a single abstract method (e.g., Runnable, Predicate). Default and Static Methods in Interfaces: Allows method definitions inside interfaces. Optional Class: A container object used to avoid null references. New Date and Time API: Immutable and thread-safe classes like LocalDate, LocalTime.
